frameworks.name: approximate object representation (body vs shape)
frameworks.one_line: simplified body approximations support physical reasoning, distinct from fine-grained shape representations

frameworks.name: intuitive physics / mental simulation
frameworks.one_line: people predict physical behavior using engineer-style real-time simulation approximations

frameworks.name: convex-hull approximation
frameworks.one_line: body representation lies between convex hulls and fine-grained shapes

frameworks.name: classical psychophysics (causality, collision, change detection)
frameworks.one_line: three classic paradigms repurposed to dissociate body from shape

core_question: Do people rely on simplified body approximations rather than fine-grained shape when reasoning about physical behavior of objects?
key_assumptions: tracking/action and recognition use distinct object representations
real-time physical prediction requires principled approximations
body and shape can be dissociated experimentally
manipulations: task type (causality perception, collision detection, change detection)
settings designed to dissociate body from shape
stimulus geometry varying between convex hulls and fine-grained shapes
key_findings: behavior across tasks indicates reliance on approximate bodies for physical reasoning
the body approximation lies between convex hulls and fine-grained shapes
body and shape representations dissociate across the three tasks
interpretations: physical reasoning uses an approximate body representation distinct from recognition shape
human approximations parallel engineer-developed real-time simulation models
